title: Administrative Management and General Management Consulting Services

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541611 ($114,205,171,465) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541611 ($205,640,681,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $260,722 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541611); class <5: 79,966 firms, avg revenue/firm $344,991 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $53,991 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 5,880 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,598,754 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $250,205 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 3,308 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,199,427 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $500,710 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 2,992 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,214,358 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,442,047 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 861 firms, avg revenue/firm $32,354,620 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,063,498 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 507 firms, avg revenue/firm $202,449,895 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $31,683,409 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 3,853
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

breakdown_by_role.role: Consultants and analysts (13-1111 Management Analysts + 13-1199 Business Operations Specialists + 13-2011 Accountants & Auditors + 13-1071 HR Specialists)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Research, benchmarking synthesis, data analysis, slide and deliverable production, and report drafting are among the most AI-exposed white-collar task sets. Current AI handles the analyst production layer (data pulls, chart builds, draft narratives, framework application) at substantial quality. Persisting: client interviews, workshop facilitation, political navigation inside the client organization, and judgment on ambiguous problems requiring institutional context. Fraction 0.45 reflects a large but not dominant automatable slice — the 0.4-0.6 hint range, set at the midpoint-low given the heterogeneous mix of sub-roles (some are more compliance/HR-specialist work that automates less than pure strategy analysis).

breakdown_by_role.role: Partners, principals and firm management (11-1021 General and Operations Managers)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: The partner/principal layer in consulting IS the revenue engine: rainmaking, client-executive trust, engagement leadership, and judgment calls on ambiguous problems. Only the internal reporting, admin and self-management slice automates. Fraction 0.08 reflects a minimal but nonzero automatable slice (internal dashboards, firm-level reporting, partner scheduling).

breakdown_by_role.role: Marketing and business development (13-1161 Market Research Analysts + 41-3091 Sales Representatives)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Thought-leadership content, proposals and RFP responses automate heavily with current LLMs. Relationship-based selling — executive networking, conference presence, referral cultivation — persists. Fraction 0.30 reflects that roughly a third of effort is production/drafting work that AI handles.

breakdown_by_role.role: Project and engagement management (13-1082 Project Management Specialists)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Workplans, status decks, budget tracking and meeting summaries automate. Client steering-committee management, risk escalation judgment and stakeholder alignment persist. Fraction 0.30.

Implementation tooling, analytics builds, and internal systems — AI-augmented for code generation and documentation. Concentrated in operations/digital-transformation practices; design and architecture judgment persist. Fraction 0.35.

breakdown_by_role.role: Client service and support staff (43-4051 Customer Service Representatives)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Scheduling, engagement administration, document production, and client-portal management are near-fully automatable with current AI. The small residue is escalation handling and relationship-adjacent tasks. Fraction 0.80.

breakdown_by_role.role: Unallocated tail (remainder beyond listed top-10 occupations, ~47.3% of wage bill)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: The top-10 SOC codes sum to only 52.7% of the 5416 wage bill; the remainder is dominated by additional analyst-pattern delivery labor (junior consultants, research associates, sector analysts — titles that map poorly to SOC but are high AI-exposure) plus back-office finance/admin (also high) with a small slice of senior specialist roles (lower). Blended fraction 0.45, matching the explicit analyst group but accounting for the back-office high-automation component lifting the tail average above pure analyst level.

pi_dist.rationale: Client relationship control is weak at boutique/mid-market scale for two compounding reasons. First, key-person portability: in sub-$25M consulting firms the client book is personal to the founding partner(s) — deal structures for consulting M&A universally include heavy earnouts and lockups precisely because relationship value walks with the person, not the firm entity. Second and more important for C: billing is effort-anchored (hourly, daily, or fixed-fee benchmarked to expected analyst hours) at the boutique tier — there is no documented shift to value/outcome pricing at sub-$25M revenue outside the top strategy brands. When AI compresses analyst hours per deliverable, procurement departments and competing boutiques reprice engagements down, passing AI savings to clients rather than operators retaining them as margin. A small upward offset: some retained-advisory and subscription-retainer billing models exist even at boutique scale, and proprietary industry data / frameworks create modest stickiness. Net: pi_dist 0.38, reflecting substantially deflated capture due to effort-anchored pricing plus personal rather than institutional client ownership.

pi_moat.rationale: Barrier to substitution is low-moderate for boutique management consulting. Against the firm: no licensure requirement; very low formal entry barriers (any ex-partner with a laptop competes); and a direct AI-bypass threat that is currently materializing — corporate strategy, procurement, and finance teams equipped with frontier LLMs and agentic AI can increasingly perform the research, synthesis, and deck-production work they previously bought externally. The solo/micro tail (80k of 93.5k firms under 5 employees) armed with AI becomes additional price-setting competition. In favor: executive-cover value ('we hired outside experts'), accountability for recommendations, sector-specific proprietary frameworks, facilitation capability, and the implicit political function consulting serves in organizations (external validation of decisions already reached internally). The moat rating is below 0.40 because the analyst-pyramid leverage model — which is the economic structure being sold at the in-band $1-10M EBITDA tier — is being directly eroded by AI access at the client level.

t50_years.derivation: current_adoption_pct declared at 0.30 (30% of US boutique/mid-market management consulting firms materially using AI in automatable functions — research, analysis, deck production — as of mid-2026). Basis: Hinge Research Institute 2024 Technology Adoption Report for professional services finds ~65% of consulting firms use AI tools, but 'material use in automatable functions' (production-quality AI output replacing or substantially augmenting analyst deliverables) is a higher bar estimated at 30% for the boutique/mid-market tier given lagged adoption relative to large-firm early movers. Historical analog: internet/web tools reached 50% of consulting firms in approximately 8-10 years from ~1996 commercial adoption to ~2004-2006 majority penetration; specialized software (CRM, project management, analytics platforms) similarly ran 8-10 years to majority. AI is arriving faster due to zero-friction SaaS delivery and obvious ROI, supported by the Hinge finding of high awareness and intent. Derivation: gap from 30% to 50% is 20pp; at an accelerated diffusion pace of ~5pp/year (faster than historical analogs due to the ease of AI tool access), t50 ≈ 4 years from today (2026 → 2030). Plausible range 3-6 years depending on whether adoption accelerates further or hits organizational resistance at mid-size boutiques.

current_adoption_pct.source: Hinge Research Institute 2024 Technology Adoption and AI Report for Professional Services (primary survey of consulting and professional services firms including boutiques)
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: Hinge 2024 Technology Report: ~65% of professional-services firms report using AI tools; however, 'material AI use in automatable functions' at production quality (replacing analyst drafting, research synthesis, deliverable production) estimated at 30% for the sub-$25M boutique/mid-market consulting tier, discounting for the gap between 'AI tools used occasionally' and 'AI systematically embedded in delivery workflow for automatable functions.' Material = AI actively performing automatable delivery tasks (research synthesis, report drafting, slide production, benchmark analysis) in routine client engagements, not pilots or executive experimentation only.

historical_analogs.value: Internet/web tools took approximately 8-10 years from ~1996 commercial adoption to ~2004-2006 majority consulting-firm penetration; specialized analytics and project-management software similarly ran 8-10 years to majority. AI arrives faster due to zero-friction SaaS, but mid-size boutiques show organizational friction in systematically embedding AI into delivery workflows versus large firms that can mandate it. Analog central estimate: 8 years to 50%, compressed to ~4 years for AI given accelerated starting pace.
historical_analogs.source: Hinge Research Institute High Growth Study (professional services technology adoption series, multiple years); McKinsey & Company Technology Adoption research (secondary)
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: Hinge multi-year tracking of professional-services technology adoption shows 8-10 year cycles from tool availability to majority-firm penetration for CRM, analytics platforms, and content marketing technology. AI compressing this to 4 years is an ESTIMATE given current evidence of faster diffusion pace.

owners_60plus_pct.source: Federal Reserve Small Business Credit Survey 2025 (national small-business owner demographics); McKinsey Institute for Economic Mobility 2026 succession-wave analysis; adjusted for consulting sector founder cohort
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: McKinsey 2026 succession analysis: 'more than half of U.S. small-business owners are over age 55; one in four are 65 or older.' Fed SBCS 2025: '62% of owners report accelerated retirement timelines.' For management consulting boutiques specifically: many in-band firms (20-499 employees) were founded by partners who left Big-4 or MBB in the 1990s-early 2000s; founders starting at age ~35 in 1995-2005 are now ~55-65, placing a substantial cohort in or entering the 60+ range. Estimated 38% of owners aged 60+, somewhat above the national median small-business rate but below the succession-wave peak for older-sector industries, reflecting consulting's partial draw of second-career founders.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: McKinsey 2026 Great Ownership Transfer report documents a broad small-business succession shortage: 'fewer than 30% of family-owned businesses successfully transfer to the next generation; the majority of owners lack an identified successor.' Consulting Success 2024 State of Consulting survey notes 'exit planning is the top concern for owners over 55, with 67% reporting no formal succession plan.' No consulting-specific succession-shortage study found, but the national evidence is strong and consulting's relationship-dependent value (which is hard to transfer) amplifies the shortage relative to asset-based businesses. Documented at national level with consulting-relevant amplifier; value: true.

active_consolidators.source: PE/M&A deal-tracker coverage: Equiteq knowledge-economy M&A database (consulting firm deals, 2024-2026); GF Data / PitchBook lower-middle-market consulting add-on activity
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: Named PE-backed platforms actively doing 541611-relevant add-ons in the last 24 months: (1) Ankura Consulting (Tri-Artisan Capital); (2) Maine Pointe (JMB Capital); (3) West Monroe Partners (TA Associates); (4) Riveron Consulting (Crossplane Capital); (5) Guidehouse (Veritas Capital); (6) Portage Point Partners (private-equity-backed restructuring/advisory); (7) The Hackett Group (active acquirer); (8) Protiviti adjacents and boutique add-ons (various sponsors). Count conservative at 8 dedicated to the general-management/administrative consulting sub-segment; broader advisory-services platforms (including Big-4 spin-outs and strategy adjacents) increase count but are partially out of 541611 scope. Equiteq reports 'consulting M&A activity at sector-level was healthy in 2024-2025 with PE representing approximately 55-65% of disclosed transactions in the knowledge-economy segment.'

buy_mult.source: Equiteq Consulting Firm Valuation report 2024; GF Data lower-middle-market advisory-services transactions; secondary: MergerMarket and CapIQ deal comps for sub-$10M EBITDA consulting firms
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Equiteq 2024 valuation benchmarks for knowledge-economy firms: small consulting firms ($1-5M EBITDA) typically trade at 4x-5x EBITDA after normalization for owner comp; median approximately 4.5x for single-location generalist boutiques. GF Data lower-middle-market advisory services: median EV/EBITDA 4.3x for sub-$5M EBITDA transactions 2023-2024. EBITDA normalized for market-rate partner/owner compensation per rule 3 — SDE-basis quotes excluded. Note: headline multiples in consulting often include earnouts that inflate stated multiples by 1-2x vs cash-at-close; 4.5x represents the normalized cash-at-close equivalent.

exit_mult.source: Equiteq Consulting Firm Valuation report 2024; GF Data platform-tier advisory-services transactions; PitchBook PE exit data for $10M+ EBITDA consulting platforms
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Equiteq 2024: platform-tier consulting firms ($10M+ EBITDA) with institutional client bases and demonstrated recurring revenue have transacted at 8x-10x EBITDA in 2023-2024. GF Data: median EV/EBITDA for $10M+ advisory-services platforms at 9.1x in 2024 (all-cash, post-earnout-adjusted). The wide spread between buy (4.5x) and exit (9.0x) reflects the multiple-expansion thesis of PE rollup — consolidating fragmented boutiques into an institutional platform commands a premium multiple.

pricing_structure.value: Billing mix at the boutique/mid-market management consulting tier ($1-50M revenue): approximately 60-70% daily/weekly rates or fixed-fee engagements benchmarked to expected effort (days/weeks of senior and junior consultant time); 15-20% retainer or subscription advisory (ongoing retained counsel for specific functions or C-suite access); 10-15% value/outcome-based or success-fee arrangements (predominantly at the top-of-market strategy/PE-advisory tier, rare at sub-$25M boutiques). The dominant effort-anchored billing structure means AI efficiency reducing analyst hours directly depresses the pricing of equivalent deliverables when competitors or clients can observe and bid against compressed effort costs. No documented shift to value pricing at the sub-$25M boutique tier as of 2026. Retainer and subscription models provide modest protection but represent a minority of revenue.
pricing_structure.source: Source Global Research Consulting Market Report 2024; Hinge Research Institute Professional Services Benchmark Survey 2024; Consulting Success 2024 State of Consulting
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Source Global Research 2024: 'fixed-fee and daily-rate engagements account for approximately 70% of consulting revenue across the market; outcome-based and value-pricing models, despite growing interest, remain below 20% of market revenue and are concentrated at the largest strategy firms.' Hinge 2024 benchmark: retainer/subscription arrangements reported by ~35% of professional-services firms but represent ~15-20% of revenue on average. Consulting Success 2024: most boutique owners report hourly or daily-rate as primary billing model.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 0.22711 implies a maximum EBITDA uplift of ~22.71pp of revenue, well above the 3pp threshold. A = 2.5 (t50 = 4 years) is consistent with a large prize but relatively slow diffusion in the boutique tier — the tension is appropriate: high V but suppressed A because boutique partners are slower to systematically embed AI in delivery workflows than the adoption-exposure level of the technology would imply. No inconsistency requiring revision.

terminal_value.justification: General management consulting rests on an analyst pyramid: partners sell senior judgment wrapped in junior delivery labor, charged at margin. AI is not replacing the consulting product outright — organizations will still hire outside perspectives, accountability, and executive cover — but it is dissolving the thing fees were anchored to: billable analyst effort. If AI compresses a four-week analysis to four days, the fixed-fee equivalent reprices downward to reflect competitive effort compression, not toward the client value delivered. The lever to watch: does the market shift from effort-anchored to value/outcome billing at the boutique tier? As of 2026 that shift is not documented sub-$25M. Demand survives mature AI — the judgment, facilitation, and political functions of consulting persist — but the leverage model (revenue per partner = staff pyramid * billing rate * utilization) shrinks materially as pyramid depth collapses. Classification: PRESSURED. Not MELTING because the accountability and executive-cover functions genuinely persist; not DURABLE because the economic model is under structural compression.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: Confirmed. V was built from the OEWS role mix and per-role task-automatability fractions reflecting current AI capability for research, synthesis, deck production and delivery work; A was built from the Hinge Technology Adoption survey current_adoption_pct and internet/software historical analog diffusion timelines. The active_consolidators count (8) and buy/exit multiples (4.5x / 9.0x) were used exclusively for CFD within B and M respectively; deal activity was never referenced as evidence of AI value or adoption speed.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing mix: ~65% daily/fixed-fee effort-anchored; ~18% retained advisory; ~12% value/outcome; ~5% other. The dominant effort-anchored billing structure is the primary deflation channel: when AI compresses analyst hours per deliverable, procurement departments and competing boutiques (including solo practitioners with AI leverage) reprice fixed-fee equivalents downward. AI savings flow to clients, not operators. This is fully reflected in pi_dist = 0.38 (low firm-side capture, mechanism stated: effort-anchored billing hands AI efficiency through as pricing pressure; personal rather than institutional client ownership removes the relationship stickiness that would allow fee holding). pi_moat = 0.35 carries the additional deflation from the AI-bypass threat — clients' own AI-equipped staff can now perform the analysis/deck work previously purchased.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A = 10/t50: t50=3 gives A=3.33, t50=6 gives A=1.67 — S ranges approximately 4.14 to 3.67 across this range (largest single lever). Key driver: whether the boutique tier systematically embeds AI in delivery workflows in 3 years or takes 6; the Hinge 65% tool-use figure likely overstates material production-workflow adoption.

C = 10 * pi_dist * 0.35: at 0.28 C = 0.98, at 0.50 C = 1.75 — S ranges approximately 3.71 to 4.24. Driven by whether effort-anchored billing fully deflates capture or whether retained-advisory growth allows partial decoupling.
V_raw 0.207-0.248, V 8.27-9.91; S moves roughly ±0.07. Tail composition (analyst-pattern vs back office vs specialist) is unobserved at 6-digit.

M = 4*(9.0-buy)/buy: buy=3.5 gives M=7.29, buy=5.5 gives M=2.55 — S ranges approximately 3.58 to 4.46. Consulting M&A headline multiples include earnout inflation; cash-at-close normalization critical.

reviewer_note: S=3.94, a below-median screen score: the combination of very high V (59.2% labor share, 38.4% automatable fraction — the analyst-pyramid labor structure is highly AI-exposed) and strong B (3,853 in-band targets, meaningful ownership wave) is substantially suppressed by very low C (1.33 — effort-anchored billing hands AI savings to clients, and the book is personal not institutional) and moderate A (2.5 — boutique diffusion slower than the technology's readiness would suggest). M = 4.0 reflects a real but modest 4.5x-9.0x multiple arc, penalized by key-person portability (earnout-heavy deal structures, cash-at-close lower than headline). Heterogeneous = true: NAICS 541611 spans strategy, operations, PMO, compliance and generalist advisory; the in-band ($1-10M EBITDA) firms are dominated by generalist boutiques and operations-advisory practices at 20-499 employees — these are the scored sub-segment, but strategy-pure and MBB-adjacent firms within the code would score differently (higher C, lower A). Formula result is directionally consistent with intuition: high AI exposure, but weak capture and slow boutique diffusion yield a middling screen score. No override. Key conflicts: (1) Source Global Research and Hinge survey frames span the full consulting market including Big-4/MBB that distort AI adoption rates upward; boutique-specific data is scarce. (2) Buy/exit multiples are sourced from secondary republication of Equiteq and GF Data; direct primary access not confirmed, quality MED. (3) Damodaran 15.65% margin used for n_band vs Service Leadership-style consulting benchmarks suggesting 8-22% range — n_band estimate is sensitive to margin assumption but stays in the 2,000-5,000 range across plausible margins. (4) owners_60plus_pct = 0.38 is a calibrated estimate; consulting-specific owner-age data not found from a primary named survey. (5) The succession_shortage_documented flag is set true on strong national evidence plus a consulting-amplifier argument (relationship value is harder to transfer than asset value); the OW +0.1 bonus is applied per template rules.
